Risks

The most immediate-term risk to my thesis on Applied Digital is failing to finalize a lease agreement for the Delta Forge 1 campus by the April 18th deadline. As such a failure would accelerate the Macquarie loan, the company would have to pay back all drawn funds, $87.1 million, in addition to accrued interest on July 18th. This could potentially impact the company’s ongoing construction efforts at its campuses and possibly draw out its timelines.

On that note, construction delays could have a major impact on the company’s lease agreement with Oracle and potentially put the $5 billion lease at risk. According to S&P, Oracle can terminate the lease agreement if the project is more than 150 days late from its phased ready-for-service dates beginning October 1st, 2026.

Losing the Oracle lease could be detrimental for the company’s prospects as an AI landlord as it could lose hyperscalers’ trust in its timelines. This could be devastating for its ongoing talks with a hyperscaler for leasing 900 MW of IT load at Delta Forge 1 and two other sites. In that case, the company could be forced to seek riskier non-investment grade tenants to fill its campuses, which would raise its cost of capital and impact its potential cash flows negatively.

Conclusion

Overall, I remain bullish on Applied Digital following its FQ3 earnings, which provided a snapshot of its HPC hosting segment’s earnings upside potential after generating a 24.7% segment operating margin in its first full quarter of operations at Polaris Forge 1’s 100 MW building. These results are extremely promising, in my opinion, given that the company is on track to bring an additional 600 MW of IT load online by the end of FY 2027.

Earnings results aside, the company’s latest lease amendments with CoreWeave significantly de-risked the lease agreement by moving it to an investment grade SPV, while securing parent level guarantees and a $50 million letter of credit. Moreover, its procurement of 600-700 MW of MEP equipment per year and building its own power plants through Base Electron could drive demand for its campuses higher due to the current bottlenecks regarding the supply of electrical equipment and grid limits.

Meanwhile, short-term catalysts worth watching include potential lease agreements with an investment grade hyperscaler for the 300 MW Delta Forge 1 campus and two other sites, as well as a new lease agreement with Oracle for the remaining 100 MW at Polaris Forge 2. Additionally, CoreWeave could exercise its option for leasing more buildings at Polaris Forge 1 to deliver on its latest major deals with Meta and Anthropic.

Given these factors, and my forecast of the company reaching its $1 billion NOI target in FY 2028, I’m reiterating my strong buy rating for Applied Digital with a $40 price target, representing 53% upside potential from its current valuation.

data.title: IBIT: Why I Stepped To The Side (Technical Analysis) (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
I have never been a big fan of Bitcoin. Yes, I appreciate blockchain, but, until recently, I had no interest in cryptocurrency. In late February, I wrote about why it makes sense to invest in iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF (IBIT), but it has rallied, and I am no longer bullish. I have downgraded the ETF from Buy to Hold.

IBIT Has Outpaced the Market

The market has been crazy since I wrote my Buy recommendation on February 23rd, as the Iranian War began five days later, on Saturday, February 28th. Since February 27th, stocks are down, and precious metals are actually down. Here is the action since the last article:

Bitcoin is still down a lot year-to-date, so perhaps the rebound is nothing but a bounce. In that Buy piece, I discussed two stocks that I have been watching closely recently that are pegged to Bitcoin: Coinbase Global (COIN) and Strategy Inc (MSTR). IBIT has outpaced both of them:

Year-to-date, each of these is down, with COIN down 25.8%, IBIT down 16.3%, and MSTR down 15.3%. SPDR Gold Shares (GLD) and iShares Silver Trust (SLV) are up in 2026 by 10.3% and 7.2%, respectively, though they are both well below their peaks.

The Direction of Bitcoin Could Go Either Way

I suggested in that last article that Bitcoin (and IBIT) could catch up to COIN and MSTR, which it has. I pointed on the chart to some open gaps, and they do remain open:

Not only are the three gaps open from January that I pointed out in that last article, but there is also a new one that was almost filled on Friday, when the high of $41.59 fell just short of the $41.61 low from March 17th. These gaps could get filled. There are also two gaps that were created last week below the current price that remain open.

IBIT tracks Bitcoin, its only asset. The ETF, which now has 35,200 followers at Seeking Alpha, has net assets of $57.7 billion as of April 10th, according to the iShares website. The fund launched in early 2024 and still trades well above the all-time low of $22.02 set early in that year. Bitcoin, while sharply lower in 2026 and down over the past year, is still in a long-term upward trend:

While Bitcoin has retreated sharply, it is still up about 915% since the end of 2019, a move that has far outpaced precious metals. Yes, it could keep retreating, as I suggested was a risk before, and it could keep bouncing too.

The world is worried about the U.S. dollar, though it has not moved that much this year, and Bitcoin (and IBIT) could benefit from those concerns if they accelerate. Traditionally, gold has received interest for its ability to store wealth and protect from currency devaluations and inflation, but cryptocurrency can do so as well.

A development that I think is favorable is that Wall Street seems to be opening up to the idea of cryptocurrency as an investment. The CFA Institute has shared information about cryptocurrency for a couple of years, and I think more work needs to be done to help people think of Bitcoin as a potential investment rather than a gamble. It has published a lot of research since 2023 on the subject and launched a valuation guide for investment professionals in late 2023.

Risks and Opportunities for IBIT

One of the risks to Bitcoin is that advances in quantum computing could allow hackers to get around the encryption that secures it. Another risk is that governments could restrict cryptocurrency. I have seen a lot of discussion about these two risks, but I am unaware of any progress by quantum computing yet or any governments moving to restrict Bitcoin.

IBIT, which is focused exclusively on Bitcoin, faces the risk that other cryptocurrencies outpace Bitcoin. One of these is Ethereum, but there could be others.

IBIT, which is not the only ETF for Bitcoin, launched when the government cleared the path through the SEC. IBIT has been the largest ETF, but there are new ETFs launching, including one that launched last week from Morgan Stanley (MSBT). I don't think that iShares needs to be concerned at this time, as its execution has been strong and its fee reasonable at 0.25%, though there is a risk that new ETFs end up eroding the overall market for Bitcoin. Currently, the next-largest Bitcoin ETF is less than 25% the size of IBIT.

Conclusion

When I wrote that article in February, I had no position in IBIT in my ETF model portfolio, which aims to beat 60% stocks and 40% bonds. I did add some the very next day after the article, paying $36.48 for a 3% position that I ended up selling the next day at $39.07. I did add some back at $38.03 the next day in the model portfolio, a 4% position, and I ended up boosting it. I went back and forth with this position, which was a maximum of 9.2% of the model portfolio, for a while, but I exited it completely last week.

Yes, IBIT may continue to bounce, but it could also continue to retreat. When I see the action in precious metals, I get a bit discouraged. Additionally, IBIT has outpaced Coinbase and Strategy, Inc, both of which have market caps that are similar to the assets in IBIT. Predicting the future value of Bitcoin is quite challenging in my view, and I think that there could be a better opportunity to get long IBIT.

data.title: XES: Oil Service Stocks Turn Pricey; Why It's Time To Take Profits (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Oil prices soared in March following the onset of the conflict in Iran. The prompt-month contracts of both WTI and Brent crude oil neared $120 per barrel at various times, but later-dated futures didn’t jump quite as much. The market-implied reality is that the war in the Middle East may be short-lived, with the Strait of Hormuz reopening within weeks (maybe months).

So, shares of energy services companies might have a narrow window to thrive with near-triple-digit oil prices. Today, I’m downgrading the State Street SPDR S&P Oil & Gas Equip & Serv ETF (XES). I had a buy rating on the product back in December of 2024. Shares are up 40% since then, dividends included, outperforming the S&P 500 by 27 percentage points. With a higher P/E multiple but an improved chart, taking some profits is prudent.

XES: Up More Than 100% YoY, Beating the Oil ETF

According to the issuer, XES seeks to provide exposure to the oil and gas equipment and services segment of the S&P Total Market Index, which comprises the Oil & Gas Drilling sub-industry and the Oil & Gas Equipment & Services sub-industry. The fund employs a modified equal-weighted approach, which offers the potential for unconcentrated industry exposure across large-, mid-, and small-cap stocks.

XES has grown substantially since my previous analysis. Total assets under management are now $496 million, up from just $223 million in late 2024. The annual expense ratio is low to moderate at 35 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is also modest at 1.18%—near that of the S&P 500.

Share-price momentum has been stellar this year, earning the product a pristine A+ ETF Grade in that category by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. But XES is also highly risky, as evidenced by a weak D- Grade. Its historical realized standard deviation is elevated, while tight concentration on a segment of the Energy sector means the fund’s holdings will largely move in unison when volatility strikes the oil patch.

Finally, on liquidity, XES has mixed marks. Average daily volume is under 60,000 shares, while its median 30-day bid/ask spread is a bit wide at 13 basis points. So, I encourage prospective investors to use limit orders, particularly around the market open.

For an update on the portfolio, the 2-star, Bronze-rated ETF by Morningstar plots along the bottom row of the style box, indicating its material weight to small-cap stocks. I’d call out that 8.4% of the allocation is classified as non-US, so there is some geographical diversification. Moreover, there’s a decent mix between value, blend, and growth from a style point of view.

On valuation, we run into issues. The price-to-earnings ratio has climbed to above 25x. Contrast that to the P/E from December 2024—under 13x. With a long-term EPS growth rate of 11.8% (solid), the PEG ratio is now above 2x. That’s a steep price to pay for a cyclical ETF. As a result, I am downgrading XES to a hold.

XES: Valuation & Factor Profiles

Zooming in on the sector breakdown, there’s actually good single-stock diversification with this modified-equal-weight product. The top 10 assets, together, make up 42% of the portfolio.

The 100% Energy Sector ETF has paid a low dividend in the last many years, and with earnings season on the horizon, investors should carefully monitor price action leading into the key report.

XES: Holdings & Dividend Information

Seasonally, there’s not a whole lot to write home about. April and May have produced tepid price returns when scanning data over the past 10 years, with better performance occurring in June and July before August volatility.

XES: Neutral Near-Term Calendar Trends

The Technical Take

With now a premium valuation on this resource-heavy ETF, XES’s technical situation is strong. Notice in the chart below that the fund has broken out to its best level in 7 years. Given the intense momentum, I could see the price rising further to the breakdown point from 2018—near $160. Support is down near the long-term 200-day moving average and the range highs from 2023 and 2024, $84 and $100, respectively. And given that the 200dma is on the rise, it’s clear that the bulls control the primary trend.

Also take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph. It has ranged in a bullish zone between 40 and 80 since the middle of last year, confirming the bull run. And with a high amount of volume by price below today’s level, there should be ample cushion if we see a protracted pullback.

XES: $160 Upside Potential, Rising 200dma, $84 Support

The Bottom Line

While XES’s chart and momentum stand out in a strong way, the valuation is just too much to overcome. Hence, I am downgrading the oil services ETF following a massive rally since last April.

data.title: HP Inc.: Willing To Bite At A 6.5% Yield, Despite Memory Risk (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
The S&P 500 has fully recovered all of its year-to-date losses, with investors hopeful that talks with Iran will produce an end to the conflict and restore oil prices back to normal. What has not recovered, however, is tech stocks. The stock market is still confused about how to treat AI: is it a tailwind or a headwind? In the meantime, the data center building boom continues in full force, skyrocketing the price of its most constrained component: memory.

Add memory to the list of problems at HP Inc. (HPQ), the maker of PCs and printers. Despite a rebound in PC sales and slight improvement in printing trends, HP's stock has declined ~15% since the start of the year. The question for investors now is, is it an appropriate time to buy the dip, or is HP a falling knife?

I last wrote a neutral article on HP in December, when the stock was trading in the low $20s. At the time, I had cautioned that the company's withering profits in its PC division, coupled with secular tailwinds in print, would cause challenges for the company. But at the same time, a low valuation and rich dividend yield made up for those risks. Today, with HP trading much lower, the compensation from a valuation angle is much more appealing, pushing me to be cautiously optimistic on this name: I'm upgrading HP to a buy.

At current share prices, I see more potential tailwinds for this stock than headwinds. The core upside drivers are:

  • HP has returned to earnings growth. With the company effectively driving a flat business mix (PC sales strength offset by slight declines in print), the company's generous share buybacks over the past few years are finally driving HP to achieve positive earnings growth, which makes its single-digit P/E ratios look off.
  • Strength in PC unit sales. HP has recently accelerated to double-digit unit sales growth in PCs, which is notable in a tougher consumer economy. The company benefits from having a broad portfolio of PC products at all price points (though we note that the recent sunsetting of Windows 10 may also have accelerated upgrade cycles).
  • Deploying AI to cut costs. Fresh off a 3-year transformation plan, the company has identified a further ~$1 billion in annualized opex savings through AI.
  • Rich dividend. The company pays $0.30 per quarter, which at current share prices translates to a hefty 6.5% yield.

To me, valuation is the core reason to buy into HP. It's true that the company recently issued cautious updates to its outlook for FY26, which is the year that ends in October this year. The company still believes it can grow revenue faster than the PC and print industries, but it added a note that it expects growth rates to moderate in the back half of the year.

Also, while HP has maintained its earnings ranges at $2.90-$3.20 in pro forma EPS and $2.8-$3.0 billion in free cash flow, the unexpected spike in memory prices is driving the company to believe it will land closer to the low end of this range.

At current share prices near $19, HP trades at a market cap of $17.1 billion; adding on the $6.6 billion of net debt on the company's most recent balance sheet gives us an enterprise value of $23.7 billion. If we conservatively use the low end of the company's guidance ranges this year, HP trades at:

  • 6.4x FY26 P/E
  • 8.5x EV/FY26 FCF

These are multiples that might be appropriate for an industrial company undergoing major technological shifts and severe revenue declines. While we're worried about secular headwinds in print, overall HP remains in growth mode - which is why we think these deeply discounted multiples, on top of a healthy 6.5% dividend yield, make this a stock worth buying.

I wouldn't necessarily bet a large position here, but in my view, HP is a great buy that offers both income and a chance for gains via upward re-rating.

Q1 Download

Let's now go through HP's latest quarterly results for fiscal Q1 (which is the quarter ending in January) in greater detail. First, the headline figures:

We applaud the fact that HP returned to accelerating revenue growth yet again in Q1, with as-reported revenue growing 6.9% y/y to $14.44 billion. This substantially beat Wall Street's expectations of $13.93 billion (+3.1% y/y growth) by nearly a four-point margin. On a constant currency basis, excluding the benefit of the weakening dollar, revenue accelerated 140bps y/y to 5.2% FX-neutral growth, versus 3.8% growth in Q4.

That acceleration, in turn, was primarily driven by the PC division. As a reminder, HP's PC division generates roughly two-thirds of its sales and yet only roughly one-third of its operating profit. Revenue from PCs grew 11% y/y to $10.3 billion.

Unit sales, meanwhile, increased 12% y/y, with strength across consumer (+14% y/y unit growth) as well as commercial PC sales (+11% y/y). Revenue from consumer PCs, in particular, grew 16% y/y, as consumer ASPs also pushed higher.

As a reminder again here, Windows ended support for Windows 10 on October 14, 2025. This has been driving owners of many older PCs to upgrade to new devices, which may have caused a "pull in" impact on demand. That said, this observation seems to already be captured in HP's guidance, which calls for growth rates to moderate in the second half of FY26.

Where the core risk lies is in operating profits in the PC division, which fell -50bps y/y to 5.0%. Despite double-digit revenue growth, adjusted operating profit in nominal dollar terms in the PC segment grew only 0.8% y/y to $511 million, reflecting the headwind that HP is facing from sharply higher memory costs.

The print division, meanwhile, continued its secular declines. Revenue was down -2% y/y, though the -3% decline (in constant currency terms) improved slightly from a -4% decline in Q4. But here as well, we note that operating margins fell -40bps y/y due to tariff-related cost spikes.

To protect its margins, management notes that it has secured memory supply agreements through the end of FY26 (increasingly, memory vendors like Micron (MU) are asking customers to place longer-term commitments and backlog throughout the shortage period). Per CEO Enrique Lores' remarks on these actions on the Q1 earnings call:

First, on the supply side, we have leveraged the strength of our supplier relationships and secured long-term agreements covering our memory requirements for fiscal '26. We've qualified new suppliers built in strategic inventory positions for key platforms and cut the time to qualify new material in half to accelerate our product configuration changes.

Second, on the cost side, we've expanded lower-cost sourcing across our commodity basket, lowering logistics costs with agile end-to-end planning processes as part of the company-wide AI-enabled program. And we've accelerated company-wide productivity efforts as part of this program to use the company-wide broader cost base to execute offset.

Third, at the same time, we are implementing strategies on supply and cost. We are also configuring our products and shaping demand to align the supply we have with our customer needs. And we are taking targeted pricing actions to offset the remaining cost impact in close partnership with both our channel and direct customers. We have a strong track record of managing through commodity super cycles and uncertain times."

Importantly, however, we note that HP is still capable of supporting its capital returns program, though the margin of error is small. In Q1, the company spent $602 million on total shareholder returns, which comprises $277 million in dividends and $325 million in buybacks.

Against the company's $2.8 billion low-end FCF expectations for the year, the company's annualized dividend burden of ~$1.1 billion represents only a small 39% payout ratio. Inclusive of buybacks at the pace HP set in Q1 ($602 million in combined capital returns), that payout ratio rises to 86%. It's on edge, but as long as HP can continue to mitigate memory price increases with opex efficiencies and maintain low-single-digit overall sales growth, the company's dividends are secure.

Risks and Key Takeaways

Of course, with a stock trading so cheaply, we need to be crystal clear on what the risks are. Needless to say, we could continue to see memory prices skyrocket beyond the FY26 period in which HP has already secured supply. On top of this, HP continues to face secular headwinds in printing and will eventually need to generate more profit in its PC division to offset the contribution from print.

That said, with HP trading at single-digit P/E and FCF multiples, I think we have a de-risked entry point that helps to compensate for HP's operating risks. In my view, it's time to buy this stock.

data.title: Mama's Creations Is Priced For Perfection - Earnings Preview
data.description:
Nobody has a perfect track record when it comes to investing. Having said that, in most instances, I turn out to be right. But every so often, the market disagrees with me, sometimes by quite a bit. A good example of this can be seen by looking at Mama's Creations (MAMA), a food-centric business that focuses on producing and selling fresh, deli-prepared foods. Its products can be found in more than 12,000 grocery chains, mass sellers, clubs, and convenience stores all throughout the US. This is not a fast-growing market. But management has done a fantastic job of expanding the business in recent years.

In my last article about the company, published in September of last year, I reaffirmed it as a ‘sell’ candidate. It's not that I had a problem with the overall fundamentals of the business. In fact, the firm was growing at a rather rapid pace. My problem, instead, was just how expensive the stock was. Yes, management was forecasting significant growth over the next few years and has delivered on some of that expansion. But unless you expect everything to go perfectly, the end result here is a business that is drastically overpriced. Shares are up 72.2% since then, drastically outperforming the 5.2% increase of the S&P 500 over the same window of time.

Looking at the picture again, I see an enterprise that is doing quite well. I can understand why investors are enthusiastic. However, the stock is still incredibly pricey. And unless you expect rapid growth without meaningful additional debt, I don't really see a realistic scenario where the company comes out looking like a good prospect. Because of this, I firmly believe that maintaining it as a ‘sell’ candidate makes sense. But with earnings around the corner and analysts expecting a big improvement in revenue and profitability, I am willing to admit that I could be wrong if the data justifies a change in opinion.

A Wild Ride for Mama's Creations

Operationally speaking, I would say that Mama's Creations is doing a really good job right now. Results for the first nine months of the 2026 fiscal year as an example. Management reported revenue of $117.7 million. This represents an increase of 31.2% over the $89.7 million that the business reported a year earlier. According to the company, this rise in sales was the result of higher volumes as the company introduced new products that it sold to existing customers, as well as certain trade and marketing promotions. New customers coming on also aided the business, as did the firm's purchase of Crown 1 Enterprises, which operates as a full-service producer of value-added proteins and ready-to-eat meats that the company acquired from Sysco Corporation (SYY) in early September in exchange for $17.5 million. That alone added $10 million to the top line of the company thanks to its recently upgraded and expanded 42,000-square-foot USDA-certified production facility based out of New York.

Speaking of new products, management has been very active in catering to its clientele. This includes its branded roasted chicken strips, chicken-stuffed meatballs, roasted vegetable offerings, expanded international flavors such as Korean beef BBQ meatballs, as well as teriyaki chicken and fried rice. Management continues to invest in certain growth prospects of an organic nature. I already covered these in my previous work on the business. But they consist of a 19,000-square-foot expansion to an already existing 24,000-square-foot facility out of New Jersey and construction work to double its grilled chicken capacity and to increase usable space by 10,000 square feet at a facility in New York.

As revenue for the company improves, profitability increases also. Net income jumped from $2.1 million in the first nine months of 2025 to $3.1 million in the same time in 2026. Operating cash flow jumped from $6 million to $8.2 million. And if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $5.1 million to $8 million. Finally, EBITDA for the business expanded from $7 million to $9.9 million. If we project out results based on what we have seen already for 2026, we would get net profits for the year of $5.4 million. Adjusted operating cash flow would be about $10.4 million, while EBITDA for the business would come in at just a hair under $13 million.

In the chart above, you can see how the company is priced on both an absolute basis and relative to other similar companies. These are, quite honestly, astronomically high trading multiples. I typically would expect a firm like this to trade well below what it is now. The chart knows how the business stacks up against five other similar companies. On a price-to-operating cash flow basis, these companies are all cheaper than it is. Meanwhile, on an EV-to-EBITDA basis, only one of the businesses is more expensive.

Market participants have rewarded the company not only for the rapid growth the company has achieved but also for the expectation of further expansion over the next few years. By focusing on acquisitions, organic growth, and new product innovation, the firm believes that by the year 2030 it will grow to generate $1 billion a year in revenue. This is possible. But if analysts are correct about the final quarter of the 2026 fiscal year, overall sales for the year should be about $170.4 million. That means that the company will have to achieve annualized revenue growth of 55.7% for the 2027 through 2030 fiscal years. Organic growth can do a lot. But almost certainly acquisitions will have to play a meaningful role.

As part of my analysis, I wanted to see what the company might look like if it does actually achieve $1 billion a year or more in revenue. The five companies that I compared it to generate around that much in sales each year. In the chart above, you can see what their overall profitability margins are, specifically their EBITDA margins. The average here would be about 12.44%. Applying that to Mama's Creations would result in EBITDA of about $124.4 million. If we scale up adjusted operating cash flow at a similar rate, we would get a reading of $100 million.

At that point, the firm would be trading at a price-to-operating cash flow multiple of about 6.3 and at an EV-to-EBITDA multiple of 5. This is on a forward basis, which means that I am using current pricing for the company with future cash flows extending through the 2030 fiscal year. That certainly looks cheap, but when you consider that the average price to operating cash flow multiple of these companies is 7.6 while the average EV to EBITDA multiple (excluding one clear outlier) is 9, this does not really leave significant upside unless things go exceptionally well.

If the company trades at those multiples out into the future, that would mean that shareholders would have an annualized upside of just 4.6% using the priced operating cash flow approach. And in the event that we use the EV to EBITDA approach, upside would be about 15.6% per annum. That is actually attractive considering that the market typically averages between 10% and 11% per year. But this particular measure of upside assumes no change in net debt. In order to grow rapidly, the firm will either have to dilute shareholders meaningfully or it will have to take on more debt. If, for instance, the business is willing to accept a net leverage ratio of 2, that would reduce the annualized upside to 8.3%. So now we are at submarket returns. When you add on top of this the fact that this path means that the company is priced for perfection, you can understand, even if you disagree with, my worries.

Of course, the picture could always change as new data becomes available. It just so happens that, on April 14th, management will be announcing results for the final quarter of the 2026 fiscal year. Leading up to that point, analysts do believe that revenue will come in at $52.6 million. That would be well above the $33.6 million the company reported a year earlier. Earnings per share should flatline at $0.04. But because of a slight change in share count, we would get an increase in net profits from $1.6 million to about $1.7 million. I would venture to say that a rise in revenue and overall profitability will translate to improved cash flows. We don't know what analysts think might be the case there. But in the table below for context, you can see how the company performed for the final quarter of 2025.

Takeaway

As much as I would love to be able to be bullish or even neutral on Mama's Creations, I am not ready to make that move just yet. I don't like when shares of companies are priced for perfection because plenty of disruptions can occur. When you add on top of this the very high likelihood that even achieving the targets will leave limited upside potential on an annualized basis, it's not difficult to understand why I would reaffirm the business as a ‘sell’ candidate.
